A HISTORY

OF THE AMERICAN PEOPLE

CHAPTER I

BETWEEN WAR AND PEACE

THE surrender of the Earl of Cornwallis at York-
town closed the career in America of the only British
general who had shown commanding gifts in the field.
It closed also the revolution itself. The southern cam-
paign had been undertaken at the wish and upon the
plan of Cornwallis. He knew how pronounced division
of opinion was in the South, and how certain a hold
could be got there upon the sympathy of scores of men
who could be of the utmost service to an army in the
field. He knew how important it was to close the south-
ern ports, and particularly the Chesapeake, in order
to shut out the supplies that came steadily in from
over sea. He believed that an effectual blow struck
from the south at Virginia, after the reduction of the
far southern ports, would secure for the British an al-
most commanding position on the continent. Had
Clinton helped him promptly, instead of grudging
him aid; had he been enabled to move rapidly and in
force as he had planned, he might have accomplished

I

III.-1
